params.text:
CQC notifications and being open

Alongside RIDDOR, the CQC requires registered providers to send statutory notifications about certain events. These include the death of a person using the service, serious injuries, abuse or allegations of abuse, and events that stop the service running safely.

The duty of candour (Regulation 20) requires registered persons to be open and honest when something goes wrong and causes, or could cause, harm. When a notifiable safety incident occurs, the service must:

  • Tell the person affected (or their representative) in person as soon as reasonably practicable.
  • Give a truthful, step-by-step account of what happened.
  • Apologise — saying sorry is the right thing to do and is not an admission of legal liability.
  • Explain what will be done next and offer reasonable support.

params.alt: Illustration for CQC & duty of candour

params.question: Under the duty of candour, apologising to a person after something has gone wrong counts as admitting legal liability.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Saying sorry is required by the duty of candour and is not an admission of liability.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. An apology is an act of openness and compassion; it is not, in itself, an admission of legal liability.
